title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an open and enhanced collection of musical elements suitable for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
To understand the 2010s music landscape, we utilized the MusicOSet dataset, which provides an open and enhanced collection of musical elements suitable for data mining. Our analysis relied on three primary files:

hits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 11,959 songs that appeared on the Billboard Hot 100.
nonhits_dataset.csv: Spotify audio features for 899,068 songs by “hit artists” (artists that had at least one charting song) that did not chart.
artists.csv: Metadata for 11,518 artists, including their associated genres.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
The Cleaning Process

1. Broadening the Genre Categories
In the artists dataset, the main_genre entries were often too specific/niche for effective usage We used keyword searches to group these into broader categories such as ‘Pop’, ‘Hip Hop/Rap’, ‘Rock/Metal’, and ‘Electronic/Dance’, among others. Any genres with fewer than 100 associated artists were moved into an ‘Other’ category to maintain statistical significance.


2. Standardizing the 2010s Scope
We applied several filters to our hits and nonhits datasets:

Timeframe: We only kept tracks with a release date between 2010 and 2019, since this is the scope of interest. Since the provided release_date was based on when the song was uploaded to Spotify, artists whose older music (typically made before the streaming era) was added to Spotify during this period were also removed.
Consistency: We dropped songs by artists who did not have at least one hit during this specific decade (to create two comparable datasets where popularity of the arist during this period is less of a factor).
Outlier/Missing Artist Removal: We identified and removed certain “artists” who uploaded extremely long mixes of multiple songs (like workout mixes), which skewed our duration_ms column. We also removed artists that were not found in the artists dataset, and thus wouldn’t have been able to be assigned to a genre.



3. Balancing the Dataset
The raw data was heavily skewed toward non-hits (nearly 900,000 non-hits vs. 12,000 hits). To create a fair classification model (one that wouldn’t succeed by guessing “not a hit” everytime), we:

Took a random sample of non-hits equal to the number of hits.
This resulted in a final combined_music_data set of 8,132 songs (4,066 hits and 4,066 non-hits).



4. Normalization
To ensure our model wasn’t influenced by the different scale for certain measurements, we normalized multiple key variables—loudness, tempo, and duration_ms—onto a scale of 0 to 1.

title: Data Sources and Cleaning
text:
Final Dataset Structure and Codebook
After cleaning, our dataset consisted of 8,132 observations across 16 variables. Below is the summary of our primary explanatory variables after normalization, as well as what they mean and our interpretation of their summary statistics. Unless stated otherwise, 1 indicates a high level of the variable and 0 indicates a low level.










Variable
Description
Mean
Std. Dev.
Interpretation




duration_ms
Track length, standardized (0 to 1).
0.085
0.032
Standardized values reflect a narrow range, indicating high consistency in track length.


acousticness
Confidence measure of whether a track is acoustic.
0.329
0.331
Low mean suggests a dataset dominated by electric or synthesized production.


danceability
Suitability for dancing based on tempo, rhythm, and beat.
0.578
0.161
Most tracks lean toward being danceable, aligning with mainstream chart trends.


energy
Perceptual measure of intensity and activity.
0.590
0.232
Songs generally lean toward high intensity with moderate variation.


instrumentalness
Likelihood that a track contains no vocals.
0.119
0.279
Low mean confirms the vast majority of tracks are vocal-heavy.


liveness
Presence of an audience in the recording.
0.210
0.191
Indicates that studio recordings are significantly more prevalent than live versions.


loudness
Overall loudness in decibels (dB), standardized (0 to 1).
0.857
0.077
Very high mean and low SD show tracks are consistently mastered at similarly high volumes.


speechiness
Presence of spoken words in a track.
0.082
0.104
Confirms the dataset consists almost entirely of music rather than spoken word.


valence
Musical positiveness (cheerfulness/euphoria).
0.506
0.247
Shows a balanced mix of “happy” and “sad” emotional tones across the decade.


tempo
The pace of the track (BPM), standardized (0 to 1).
0.577
0.139
A healthy, wide distribution covering various speeds and rhythms.


explicit
Binary indicator for explicit lyrical content.
0.140
0.347
Only 14% of the sampled tracks contain explicit content labels.



Not Included:

key: estimated overall key of the track, maps pitches to integers using standard Pitch Class notation (-1 = no key detected, 0 = key of C, …, 11 = key of B).
mode: indicator of what kind of scale was used (1 = major scale, 0 = minor scale)
genre: genre that the main artist of a track is associated with
hit: boolean indicating if a song reached the Billboard Hot 100

title: MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science Project
text:
Hello! We are the Danish Club, made up of Vassar College senior mathematics majors Anders Hansen and Miles Sondergaard Jensen. Welcome to our MATH 244: Intermediate Data Science final project report. Navigate the tabs at the top to explore our analysis of what separates hit songs from non-hit songs!





Image from rawpixel

title: Introduction
text:
In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its tangible musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like marketing, catchiness, pure luck, or timing, among others?
Our project seeks to address this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 at any point after its release


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons:

Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, making the music personally resonant.
Lasting Impact: The music from this era was exceptionally high-quality, with many tracks still charting or receiving many streams today.
Shifting Trends: The criteria for what makes a song “successful” appeared to undergo significant shifts during this ten-year window.




The primary objective of this study is to predict the success of 2010s songs based on their specific audio characteristics. We aimed to build a classification model capable of differentiating hit songs from non-hit songs with at least 50% accuracy (better than just random guessing!).



Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

Predictors of Success: We hypothesized that higher danceability, valence (musical positiveness), and loudness would significantly increase a song’s likelihood of becoming a hit. Conversely, we expected lower duration, acousticness, speechiness, and instrumentalness to be associated with chart success.
Neutral Elements: We predicted that features like key, tempo, mode, and whether a song is explicit would have little to no measurable effect on a song’s probability of charting.


Next, we will dive into the Data and Cleaning process to show how we narrowed down nearly a million observations into a clean, balanced dataset for our analysis and modeling.

text:
In the music industry, there is a long-standing debate: is a song’s success driven by its tangible musical characteristics, or is it a product of “unmeasurables” like marketing, catchiness, pure luck, or timing, among others?
Our project seeks to address this by analyzing a comprehensive sample of hit and non-hit songs released between 2010 and 2019. We define a “hit” specifically as any song that made an appearance on the Billboard Hot 100 at any point after its release


We chose to focus on the 2010s for several reasons:

Formative Years: This decade represents the childhood and teenage years for many in our generation, making the music personally resonant.

Going into our modeling phase, we developed two sets of expectations regarding Spotify’s audio features:

title: Modeling Results
text:
Our primary goal was to differentiate between hit and non-hit songs with better than 50% accuracy. [cite_start]Both our LASSO and Ridge models successfully exceeded this threshold, achieving approximately 67% accuracy on the test data[cite: 113, 117, 128].


Whether we used the L1 (LASSO) or L2 (Ridge) penalty, the performance remained remarkably consistent:

[cite_start]Accuracy: ~67.1%
[cite_start]F1 Score: 0.68
[cite_start]ROC-AUC: 0.73

[cite_start]Interestingly, removing “Genre” as a predictor only slightly decreased the accuracy (to about 66.5%), suggesting that the raw audio characteristics themselves carry significant weight regardless of how the song is categorized[cite: 123, 127].



The models identified several audio features that strongly influenced the likelihood of a song becoming a hit:

Instrumentalness (Negative): The strongest predictor. [cite_start]Songs with high instrumental content were significantly less likely to chart[cite: 112, 116].
[cite_start]Danceability (Positive): Higher danceability scores were consistently tied to hit status[cite: 112, 116].
[cite_start]Loudness (Positive): Consistent with the “loudness wars” of the 2010s, louder tracks had a higher probability of success[cite: 112, 116].
[cite_start]Acousticness (Negative): More “plugged-in” or produced sounds tended to perform better on the Billboard Hot 100 than acoustic tracks[cite: 112, 116].







Model
Accuracy
F1 Score
ROC-AUC




LASSO (with Genre)
67.12%
0.68
0.73


Ridge (with Genre)
67.12%
0.68
0.73


LASSO (no Genre)
66.52%
0.67
0.72



[cite_start]While our model isn’t perfect, a 67% success rate proves that there is indeed a “tangible formula” to 2010s hits that goes beyond simple marketing luck[cite: 128].

title: Methodology
text:
To predict song success, we implemented two types of regularized linear classification models: LASSO (L1) and Ridge (L2) regression. These methods were chosen specifically to handle potential multicollinearity between musical features and to prevent overfitting by penalizing large coefficients.


Our analysis was conducted using a robust Python-based pipeline integrated into our R environment via the reticulate package. The process involved several key stages:

Preprocessing: We used a column_transformer to handle different data types simultaneously.

Categorical Variables: ‘Key’, ‘Explicit’, ‘Mode’, and ‘Genre’ were processed using One-Hot Encoding.
Numerical Variables: Features like ‘Danceability’ and ‘Energy’ were passed through a StandardScaler to ensure all predictors were on the same scale.

Cross-Validation: To ensure our results were generalizable, we utilized 5-fold cross-validation during the model fitting process.
Data Splitting: The dataset was split into a Training Set (80%) to build the models and a Test Set (20%) to evaluate their final performance.




We tested both regularization techniques to see how they handled our feature set:

LASSO Regression: Useful for feature selection, as it can shrink the coefficients of less important variables all the way to zero.
Ridge Regression: Useful for dealing with highly correlated variables (like ‘Energy’ and ‘Loudness’) by shrinking coefficients proportionally without eliminating them.




We evaluated our models based on three primary metrics: * Accuracy: The percentage of correct hit/non-hit predictions. * F1 Score: The balance between precision and recall. * ROC-AUC: The model’s ability to distinguish between the two classes across various thresholds.
By comparing versions of these models with and without ‘Genre’ as a predictor, we aimed to isolate exactly how much the “sound” of a song matters versus its commercial category.
